request.question: What type of visual technique does Impossible Pictures and Beauty and the Beast have in common?

title: March of the Dinosaurs
Impossible Pictures (US)
Deep Pal
Chloë Leland
Impossible Pictures
Beauty and the Beast (1991 film)
Walking with...
Gradation (art)
Primeval: New World
List of creatures by Impossible Pictures
sentences: March of the Dinosaurs is a CGI film which has appeared on television and was released on DVD on 27 May 2011.
It was made by Impossible Pictures, the same company that created the "Walking with..." series and Prehistoric Park.
Set 70 million years ago in the Cretaceous in North America, the film follows the journey of a young "Edmontosaurus" named Scar and his herd as they migrate south for the winter.
This film depicts recent findings and speculation about Dinosaurs, such as North-American Tyrannosaurs having feathers, and hunting in packs, dinosaurs in the snow and migrating.
Impossible Pictures (Impossible) is a Denver-based production company and creative studio.
Founded by Joel Pilger in 1994 under the name Visual Approach, Inc., the company began as a 3D animation studio.
Visual Approach's first project was a 3D animated broadcast show open for the Charlotte Hornets NBA team.

Other early projects included a 3D show open/title sequence for Walt Disney Television's " ("sequel to "Beauty and the Beast)", a 3D animated campaign for JWT client Ford's "Year End Clearance", and a show open/title sequence for Universal Studios' (sequel to "Balto").
Deep Pal (born April 16, 1953) is an Indian cinematographer.
He is the son of publicist Colin Pal and grand-son of director Niranjan Pal.
Pal is known as being one of the pioneers of Steadicam camerawork in India.
Pal's first major Steadicam work was in "Shiva", which was considered a pathbreaking film on visual technique in Telugu cinema.
His later work included "Bandit Queen" directed by Shekhar Kapur and "Dil Se.
" directed by Mani Ratnam.
Chloë Leland is an award-winning writer, director, executive producer and VFX specialist.
She is also daughter of British film director and writer David Leland.
She became first notable for playing Emily Lloyd's younger sister in David Leland's directional debut "Wish You Were Here" in 1987.
She later worked for Working Title as a researcher and as a location scout on the movie "Land Girls" (1998).
Since that time she has produced, directed and shot documentaries and drama for television, including some of the highest-rated series for the Discovery Channel and Animal Planet.

She worked as camera operator on multi-award-winning feature "Concert for George" and produced and directed "Walking with Monsters", for which she won at the 58th Primetime Emmy Awards in the category "Outstanding Animated Program (For Programming One Hour or More)" and a VES as well as being nominated for a BAFTA and RTS.
Her next series, "Fight For Life", received extremely high ratings for BBC1, as well as winning BAFTA, RTS and VES awards.
In 2010 she was once again Emmy-nominated for the critically acclaimed "America – Story of Us" (the record-breaking, top rating US series), on which she worked as series VFX creative director.
She has held high-level positions with some of the UK's top production companies, such as head of development for Impossible Pictures and creative director for Jane Root's "Nutopia".
As development executive for the BBC Specialist Factual and other independent companies, Leland has originated and developed reams of commissioned series and specials.
She has written for factual and fully scripted television and authored children's books.
Impossible Pictures Ltd. is a London-based independent TV production company founded in 2002 by Tim Haines, creator of "Walking with Dinosaurs", and Jasper James.
Beauty and the Beast is a 1991 American animated musical romantic fantasy film produced by Walt Disney Feature Animation and released by Walt Disney Pictures.

The 30th Disney animated feature film and the third released during the Disney Renaissance period, it is based on the French fairy tale of the same name by Jeanne-Marie Leprince de Beaumont (who was also credited in the English version as well as in the French version), and ideas from the 1946 French film of the same name directed by Jean Cocteau.

"Beauty and the Beast" focuses on the relationship between the Beast (voice of Robby Benson), a prince who is magically transformed into a monster and his servants into household objects as punishment for his arrogance, and Belle (voice of Paige O'Hara), a young woman whom he imprisons in his castle.
To become a prince again, Beast must learn to love Belle and earn her love in return to avoid remaining a monster forever.
The film also features the voices of Richard White, Jerry Orbach, David Ogden Stiers, and Angela Lansbury.
The Walking with... series (also known as the Trilogy of Life or Walking with Prehistoric Life series) is a collection of shows that are produced by the BBC and are made by Impossible Pictures.
The aim of the series and specials, along with its books, is to recreate extinct animals and presents them as a wildlife documentary.
All the shows (with the exception of "Chased by Dinosaurs" and "Sea Monsters") focus on one individual of a certain species or that species as the main characters in each episode.
Creatures were realized through computer graphics and animatronics and are directed by Tim Haines.
The concept for the series was imagined by Tim Haines and Jasper James.
Gradation in art is a visual technique of gradually transitioning from one colour hue to another, or from one shade to another, or one texture to another.
Space, distance, atmosphere, volume, and curved or rounded forms are some of the visual effects created with gradation.
Primeval: New World is a Canadian–British science fiction television program, set in Vancouver, British Columbia, created by Judith and Garfield Reeves-Stevens.
A co-production between Impossible Pictures and Omni Film Productions for Space, the show is a spin-off of the British series "Primeval".
However, the two shows have little to do with each other.
It was broadcast on Space in Canada, and on Watch in the United Kingdom.
As with "Primeval", the premise of "Primeval: New World" involves a team who has to deal with animals from the past and future that travel through time to the present day through anomalies.
On 21 February 2013, it was announced that the series had been cancelled after a single season.

The following is a complete list of prehistoric creatures from the universe of the "Walking with..." series documentary, science fiction and fantasy television programmes, companion books and also any spin-off merchandise.
Most of the shows produced by Impossible Pictures with BBC Worldwide and Discovery Channel in association with ProSieben and France 3 and created by Tim Haines and Jasper James.
They used visual effects teams such as Framestore, The Mill and Jellyfish Pictures to bring back extinct creatures to life.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

response.hop1_query: What type of visual technique does Impossible Pictures and Beauty and the Beast have in common?
hop1_docs: Impossible Pictures (US) | Impossible Pictures (Impossible) is a Denver-based production company and creative studio.  Founded by Joel Pilger in 1994 under the name Visual Approach, Inc., the company began as a 3D animation studio.  Visual Approach's first project was a 3D animated broadcast show open for the Charlotte Hornets NBA team.  Other early projects included a 3D show open/title sequence for Walt Disney Television's " ("sequel to "Beauty and the Beast)", a 3D animated campaign for JWT client Ford's "Year End Clearance", and a show open/title sequence for Universal Studios' (sequel to "Balto").

Beautiful Agony | Beautiful Agony is a paid-subscription erotic website featuring head shots of user-submitted videos showing the participant having orgasms, without providing any visual description of what technique is being used or revealing anything below the neck and upper chest.  Both men and women are featured on the site.

Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack) | Beauty and the Beast: Original Motion Picture Soundtrack is the official soundtrack album to the 1991 Disney animated feature film, "Beauty and the Beast".  Originally released on October 29, 1991, by Walt Disney Records, the album's first half – tracks 2 to 9 – generally contains the film's musical numbers, all of which were written by composer Alan Menken and lyricist Howard Ashman, while its latter half – tracks 10 to 14 – features its musical score, composed solely by Menken.  While the majority of the album's content remains within the musical theatre genre, its songs have also been influenced by French, classical, pop and Broadway music.  Credited to Various Artists, "Beauty and the Beast: Original Motion Picture Soundtrack" features performances by the film's main cast – Paige O'Hara, Richard White, Jesse Corti, Jerry Orbach, Angela Lansbury and Robby Benson – in order of appearance.  Additionally, the album features recording artists Celine Dion and Peabo Bryson, who perform a pop rendition of the film's title and theme song, "Beauty and the Beast", which simultaneously serves as the soundtrack's only single.

Beauty and the Beast (2017 soundtrack) | Beauty and the Beast: Original Motion Picture Soundtrack is the soundtrack album to the 2017 film "Beauty and the Beast".  In the film, Belle is taken prisoner by a fearsome Beast in his enchanted castle and learns to look beyond his appearance while evading a narcissistic hunter who seeks to take Belle for himself.

Type punning | In computer science, type punning is a common term for any programming technique that subverts or circumvents the type system of a programming language in order to achieve an effect that would be difficult or impossible to achieve within the bounds of the formal language.

Panna a netvor | Beauty and the Beast is a Czech horros fairy tale film directed by Slovak film director Juraj Herz in 1978.  The film is a re-telling of the classic tale "Beauty and the Beast".  The picture is notable for its pervasive atmosphere of dread and the innovative decision to depict the beast as a bird-like creature.

Beauty and the Beast (musical) | Beauty and the Beast is a musical with music by Alan Menken, lyrics by Howard Ashman and Tim Rice, and book by Linda Woolverton.  Adapted from Walt Disney Pictures' Academy Award-winning 1991 animated musical film of the same name – which in turn had been based on the classic French fairy tale by Jeanne-Marie Leprince de Beaumont – "Beauty and the Beast" tells the story of a cold-blooded prince who has been magically transformed into an unsightly creature as punishment for his selfish ways.  To revert into his true human form, the Beast must first earn the love of a bright, beautiful young woman whom he has imprisoned in his enchanted castle before it is too late.
response.summary_1:
Header: visual/animation credits for Impossible Pictures and works titled "Beauty and the Beast"

- Impossible Pictures (US): "Founded by Joel Pilger in 1994 under the name Visual Approach, Inc." and "began as a '3D animation studio'"; did "a '3D show open/title sequence' for Walt Disney Television's (sequel to 'Beauty and the Beast)".  
- Beauty and the Beast (1991): described as the "1991 Disney animated feature film" (via its soundtrack page).  
- Ambiguity / missing fact: which "Beauty and the Beast" item is referenced by Impossible Pictures' "sequel" mention is unclear (multiple candidates: 1991 animated film; 2017 film; 1978 Czech film; stage musical). Missing fact to confirm: whether the Beauty and the Beast entry explicitly used "3D animation" (passages show "animated" for 1991 film but not explicitly "3D"). Bridge entity: Beauty and the Beast (1991 film) — verify its production/animation technique.  
- Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
  1. "Impossible Pictures (company)" — lede/History or Credits/Early projects section (confirm the cited Walt Disney Television "3D show open/title sequence" and which Beauty and the Beast sequel is meant).  
  2. "Beauty and the Beast (1991 film)" — lede/Production/Animation sections (confirm whether 3D or CGI techniques were used).  
  3. "Beauty and the Beast (disambiguation)" or "Beauty and the Beast: The Enchanted Christmas" — to resolve which sequel/variant is referenced by Walt Disney Television.  
  4. "Beauty and the Beast (2017 film)" — lede/Production if needed (to rule in/out animation vs live-action).
response.hop2_query: Beauty and the Beast 1991 film animation technique
hop2_docs: Beauty and the Beast: The Enchanted Christmas | Beauty and the Beast: The Enchanted Christmas (also known as Beauty and the Beast 2) is a 1997 American direct-to-video animated Christmas musical fantasy film produced by Walt Disney Television Animation.  It takes place within the timeline of the 1991 film "Beauty and the Beast".

Human Again (song) | "Human Again" is a song originally written for, deleted from, and later restored to the 1991 Disney animated musical "Beauty and the Beast".  With music by Alan Menken and lyrics by Howard Ashman, "Human Again" was replaced during production of the original 1991 version of the film by "Something There", but retained and revised by Menken and new lyricist Tim Rice for the 1994 stage musical adaptation of "Beauty and the Beast".  A newly produced sequence featuring "Human Again" was added to the "Beauty and the Beast" animated film for its 2002 IMAX Special Edition and subsequent DVD, VHS, and Blu-Ray home releases.

Beast (Disney) | The Beast is a fictional character who appears in Walt Disney Animation Studios' 30th animated feature film "Beauty and the Beast" (1991).  He also appears in the film's two direct-to-video followups "" and "Belle's Magical World".  Based on the hero of the French fairy tale by Jeanne-Marie Leprince de Beaumont, the Beast was created by screenwriter Linda Woolverton and animated by Glen Keane.

Beauty and the Beast (1991 film) | Beauty and the Beast is a 1991 American animated musical romantic fantasy film produced by Walt Disney Feature Animation and released by Walt Disney Pictures.  The 30th Disney animated feature film and the third released during the Disney Renaissance period, it is based on the French fairy tale of the same name by Jeanne-Marie Leprince de Beaumont (who was also credited in the English version as well as in the French version), and ideas from the 1946 French film of the same name directed by Jean Cocteau.  "Beauty and the Beast" focuses on the relationship between the Beast (voice of Robby Benson), a prince who is magically transformed into a monster and his servants into household objects as punishment for his arrogance, and Belle (voice of Paige O'Hara), a young woman whom he imprisons in his castle.  To become a prince again, Beast must learn to love Belle and earn her love in return to avoid remaining a monster forever.  The film also features the voices of Richard White, Jerry Orbach, David Ogden Stiers, and Angela Lansbury.

List of Disney's Beauty and the Beast characters | This article lists information of fictional characters from Disney's "Beauty and the Beast" franchise, covering the 1991 animated film, its direct to video , a short story collection, the stage musical adaptation, and the 2017 live-action and computer animation remake.

Belle's Magical World | Belle's Magical World (also known as Beauty and the Beast: Belle's Magical World) is a 1998 direct-to-video animated musical film produced by Walt Disney Television Animation, and released by Walt Disney Home Video) on February 17, 1998.  The film is a followup to the 1991 Walt Disney Pictures animated feature film "Beauty and the Beast", features the voices of David Ogden Stiers as Cogsworth, Robby Benson as The Beast, Gregory Grudt, who replaced Bradley Michael Pearce as Chip Potts, Paige O'Hara as Belle, Anne Rogers, who replaced Angela Lansbury as Mrs. Potts, Jerry Orbach as Lumiere.  The film features two songs performed by Belle, "Listen With Our Hearts" and "A Little Thought."  This storyline is set within the timeline of the original "Beauty and the Beast" (after Christmas but before the fight against Gaston).

Beauty and the Beast (Disney song) | "Beauty and the Beast" is a song written by lyricist Howard Ashman and composer Alan Menken for the Disney animated feature film "Beauty and the Beast" (1991).  The film's theme song, the Broadway-inspired ballad was first recorded by British-American actress Angela Lansbury in her role as the voice of the character Mrs. Potts, and essentially describes the relationship between its two main characters Belle and the Beast, specifically how the couple has learned to accept their differences and in turn change each other for the better.  Additionally, the song's lyrics imply that the feeling of love is as timeless and ageless as a "tale as old as time".  Lansbury's rendition is heard during the famous ballroom sequence between Belle and the Beast, while a shorted chorale version plays in the closing scenes of the film, and the song's motif features frequently in other pieces of Menken's film score.  "Beauty and the Beast" was subsequently recorded as a pop duet by Canadian singer Celine Dion and American singer Peabo Bryson, and released as the only single from the film's soundtrack on November 25, 1991.

response.summary_2: Both Impossible Pictures and Beauty and the Beast are associated with animation. Impossible Pictures began as a 3D animation studio and produced a 3D show open/title sequence for a Walt Disney Television Beauty and the Beast sequel. Beauty and the Beast (1991 film) is a 1991 American animated feature film produced by Walt Disney Feature Animation.
